name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِكُم مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِكُم ۖ مَّسَّتْهُمُ ٱلْبَأْسَآءُ وَٱلضَّرَّآءُ وَزُلْزِلُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَقُولَ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ مَتَىٰ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ نَصْرَ ٱللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ (٢١٤)
```

```english
Do you suppose that you will enter paradise though there has not yet come to you the like of [what befell] those who went before you? Stress and distress befell them and they were convulsed until the apostle and the faithful who were with him said, ‘When will Allah’s help [come]?’ Behold! Allah’s help is indeed near! (214)
```

## (Commentary)

It has already been mentioned that these verses, beginning from His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ادْخُلُوا فِي السِّلْمِ كَافَّةً** "O you who have faith! Enter into submission, all together" (2:208) until the end of this verse, possess a single context (سياق) that links them to one another.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ** "Do you suppose that you will enter paradise" (2:214) is a reinforcement of what the preceding verses indicated. This is the fact that religion (الدين) is a form of guidance (الهداية) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — from Allah, the Glorified, for mankind toward that which contains their felicity (السعادة) in this world and the Hereafter. It is a blessing (النعمة) with which Allah has favored them. Thus, it is obligatory that they submit to Him, not follow the footsteps of Satan (الشيطان), not introduce discord (الاختلاف) into it, not turn the remedy into a disease, and not exchange the blessing of Allah, the Glorified, for ungratefulness (الكفر) and vengeance (النقمة) by following whims (الهوى) and seeking the glitter and debris of this world. Should they do so, the wrath of their Lord will descend upon them just as it descended upon the Children of Israel when they changed the blessing of Allah after it had come to them. Indeed, the ordeal (المحنة) is perpetual and the tribulation (الفتنة) is ever-present; none among mankind shall attain the felicity of religion and proximity to the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين) except through steadfastness (الثبات) and submission (التسليم).

In this verse, there is a transition (التفات) back to addressing the believers after they were treated as being in the third person (الغيبة) in the preceding verses. The original address was with them and the speech was directed toward them in His saying: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ادْخُلُوا فِي السِّلْمِ كَافَّةً** "O you who have faith! Enter into submission, all together." The departure from this to another form was only due to a rhetorical purpose (عناية كلامية) that necessitated it; once that purpose was fulfilled, the discourse returned to addressing them a second time.

The word *am* (أَمْ) is "disjunctive" (منقطعة), signifying a digression (الاضراب). The meaning, as has been said, is: "Nay, do you suppose that you will enter paradise..." etc. The disagreement regarding the disjunctive *am* is well-known, but the truth is that *am* is used to signify hesitation or alternative (الترديد), and its indication of the meaning of digression arises from the application of the sense of digression to the specific context, not that it is a primary linguistic designation (دلالة وضعية). Thus, the meaning in this context, for example, is: Have you ceased [your previous state] by following what We commanded you of submission after faith, steadfastness upon the blessing of religion, and agreement and unity therein, or not? Nay, rather you supposed that you would enter paradise... etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَمَّا يَأْتِكُم مَّثَلُ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِن قَبْلِكُم** "while there has not yet come to you the like of [what befell] those who passed away before you?" (2:214). The word *mithl* (المِثْل) is with a *kasrah* on the *mim* and a *sukun* on the *tha*, while *mathal* (المَثَل) is with a *fathah* on both the *mim* and the *tha*, similar to *shibh* and *shabah* (likeness). What is intended by it is that which represents a thing, brings it forth, and personifies it before the listener. From this comes the term "proverb" or "parable" (المَثَل) — with two *fathahs* — which is the sentence or story that serves to bring a desired meaning to the mind of the listener by way of a representational metaphor (الاستعارة التمثيلية), as the Exalted said: **مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا التَّوْرَاةَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوهَا كَمَثَلِ الْحِمَارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفَارًا** "The example of those who were charged with the Torah, then failed to carry it, is that of an ass carrying books" (62:5). Also from this is *mathal* in the sense of an attribute (الصفة), such as His saying, the Exalted: **انظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثَالَ** "Look how they strike similitudes for you" (25:9), for they said of him صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم that he was "possessed," a "sorcerer," a "liar," and the like. Since the Exalted explains the "like" (المثل) He mentioned by His saying: **مَّسَّتْهُمُ الْبَأْسَاءُ وَالضَّرَّاءُ** "Distress and misfortune settled upon them" etc., what is intended here is the first meaning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَّسَّتْهُمُ الْبَأْسَاءُ وَالضَّرَّاءُ** "Distress and misfortune settled upon them" (2:214) to the end of the verse. When the longing of the addressee intensified to understand the detail of the general statement indicated by His saying: **وَلَمَّا يَأْتِكُم مَّثَلُ الَّذِينَ** "while there has not yet come to you the like of those," He explained it by His saying: **مَّسَّتْهُمُ الْبَأْسَاءُ وَالضَّرَّاءُ**. *Al-Ba’sa’* (البأساء) is the hardship (الشدة) directed at a person in things external to his soul, such as wealth, status, family, and the security he requires for his life. *Al-Darra’* (الضراء) is the hardship that afflicts a person within his own self, such as wounds, killing, and illness. *Al-Zalzalah* (الزلزلة) and *al-Zilzal* (shaking/convulsion) are well-known; the root is from *zalla* (زلّ), meaning he stumbled (عثر). The word is repeated to indicate recurrence, as if the earth, for instance, undergoes stumble after stumble during an earthquake. It is like *sarra* and *sarsara*, *salla* and *salsala*, *kabba* and *kabkaba*. *Al-Zilzal* in the verse is a metaphor (كناية) for agitation (الاضطراب) and being dazed (الادهاش).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **حَتَّىٰ يَقُولَ الرَّسُولُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ** "so that the Apostle and those who had faith with him said" (2:214). It has been recited with the subjunctive (نصب) for *yaqula* (يقول), and the clause in this case is the result or goal (الغاية) of what preceded it. It has also been recited with the indicative (رفع) for *yaqula*, and the clause in this case is to recount a past state (حكاية الحال الماضية). Although both meanings are correct, the second is more appropriate to the context, for the clause being a goal (غاية) that provides the reason for His saying: **وَزُلْزِلُوا** "and were shaken" does not entirely suit the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَتَىٰ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ** "‘When will Allah’s help [come]?’" (2:214). It is apparent that this is the speech of both the Messenger and those who believed with him together. There is no harm in the Messenger uttering such words as an invocation (استدعاء) and a request (طلب) for the help that Allah, the Glorified, promised His messengers and the believers in them, as the Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنَا لِعِبَادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنصُورُونَ** "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they shall indeed be the victorious ones" (37:171-172), and the Exalted said: **كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي** "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles’" (58:21). The Exalted has also said: **حَتَّىٰ إِذَا اسْتَيْأَسَ الرُّسُلُ وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ كُذِبُوا جَاءَهُمْ نَصْرُنَا** "Until when the apostles lost hope and thought that they had been called liars, Our help came to them" (12:110), which is even more intense in tone than this verse.

It is also apparent that His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَا إِنَّ نَصْرَ اللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ** "Behold! Allah’s help is indeed near" (2:214) is the speech of the Exalted Himself, not a continuation of the speech of the Messenger and those who believed with him.

The verse (as previously alluded to) indicates the permanence of the matter of trial (الابتلاء) and examination (الامتحان) and its occurrence in this nation (الأمة) just as it occurred in previous nations.

It also indicates the unity of description and likeness through the recurrence of past events, which is what is termed the repetition and return of history.
